html:
[210]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 抚军正色曰 - The military governor spoke sternly, indicating a formal and authoritative tone.
  • 外寇甫平 - External enemies have just been pacified, where '甫平' means 'just been pacified'.
  • 萑苻未靖 - Bandits in the reed marshes have not yet been pacified, where '萑苻' refers to reed marshes, often associated with bandits.
  • 商贾不通 - Merchants are not passing through, indicating economic disruption.
  • 民何聊生 - How can the people survive? Expressing the dire situation of the populace.
  • 君职司爽鸠 - Your duty is to manage the area, where '爽鸠' is a metaphor for the region or territory.
  • 坐视不理 - To sit idly by without taking action, indicating inaction.
  • 将何以辞其责也 - How can you escape your responsibility? A rhetorical question emphasizing accountability.
  • 无任主臣 - Without regard for the master-servant relationship, indicating a disregard for hierarchical duties.
  • 急檄所属府州县 - Rapidly issued an order to all prefectures, counties, and states, indicating urgency.
  • 严行缉盗 - Strictly enforced the search for bandits, indicating strict measures.
  • 如敢姑息宽纵 - If anyone dares to show leniency or indulgence, indicating strictness.
  • 立予纠劾不贷 - Immediately impeach and punish without mercy, indicating severe consequences.
  • 叠限比捕 - Repeatedly set deadlines for capturing bandits, indicating pressure.
  • 卒无所获 - Finally, nothing was captured, indicating failure.
  • 有名捕梁科者 - A famous hunter named Liang Ke, indicating a renowned figure.
  • 年七十馀 - Over seventy years old, indicating advanced age.
  • 家居柴门 - Lived in a simple cottage, indicating a modest lifestyle.
  • 久已罢役 - Had long since retired from service, indicating retirement.
  • 群捕畏比 - The hunters feared the deadlines, indicating fear of consequences.
  • 因醵资具厚币 - Therefore, they pooled money and prepared generous gifts, indicating a collective effort.
  • 遣使求助于梁 - Sent envoys to seek help from Liang, indicating a request for assistance.
  • 固辞 - Refused firmly, indicating refusal.
  • 既款使者以酒 - After treating the envoys to wine, indicating hospitality.
  • 酒酣 - When the wine was flowing freely, indicating a relaxed atmosphere.
  • 谈少年时捕盗事 - Spoke about his youth when he hunted bandits, indicating a recount of past experiences.
  • 精悍之色见于眉宇 - His sharp and vigorous appearance was evident in his eyebrows and eyes, indicating a display of vigor.
  • 使者拊掌赞叹 - The envoy clapped and praised, indicating approval.
  • 故谀词以激励之 - Used flattering words to encourage him, indicating persuasion.
  • 今盗风虽炽 - Now the banditry is rampant, indicating the current situation.
  • 均非公敌 - Are not your enemies, indicating a distinction.
  • 如勉为一行 - If you would make an effort to go, indicating a suggestion.
  • 使若曹知捕班中尚有老廉颇在 - So that you lot would know there is still an old Lian Po in the ranks of hunters, using a historical reference to Lian Po, a famous general.
  • 当不敢逞 - They would not dare to act, indicating a deterrent effect.
  • 否则若曹不谓公年老 - Otherwise, you lot would not consider you old, indicating a challenge to age.
  • 反笑公胆怯 - But would laugh at you for being cowardly, indicating mockery.
  • 当日之英雄埽地矣 - Your heroism of the past would be swept away, indicating a loss of prestige.
  • 窃为惜之 - I secretly pity you, indicating sympathy.
  • 耳热技痒 - Feeling the heat in his ears and itching in his fingers, indicating excitement and eagerness.
  • 掀髯笑曰 - Laughed and said while raising his beard, indicating a gesture of confidence.
  • 汝激吾耶 - Are you provoking me? Indicating a challenge.
  • 不得已，偕汝一行何如 - If it is unavoidable, how about going with you? Indicating a reluctant agreement.
  • 使者大喜称善 - The envoy greatly rejoiced and praised, indicating satisfaction.
  • 不日，同至山东 - In no time, they arrived together in Shandong, indicating a quick journey.
  • 群捕接见甚欢 - The hunters met him with great joy, indicating a warm reception.
  • 佥谓 - All agreed, indicating consensus.
  • 叠瞷盗踪 - Repeatedly observed the bandits' traces, indicating repeated sightings.
  • 实藏臬署 - Actually hiding in the governor's office, indicating a specific location.
  • 当侦确虚实 - Should investigate the truth, indicating a need for verification.
  • 休得孟浪 - Do not act rashly, indicating caution.
  • 乃日伏臬垣左右以伺之 - So he hid every day near the governor's office to watch them, indicating vigilance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 抚军 - Refers to a high-ranking military official, typically a governor.
  • 外寇 - Refers to external enemies or invaders.
  • 萑苻 - Refers to reed marshes, often associated with bandits or rebels.
  • 商贾 - Refers to merchants, indicating economic activity.
  • 爽鸠 - A metaphor for the region or territory, possibly referring to a specific area or administrative division.
  • 主臣 - Refers to the master-servant relationship, indicating a hierarchical structure.
  • 府州县 - Refers to prefectures, counties, and states, indicating local administrative units.
  • 捕 - Refers to hunters or law enforcement officers.
  • 梁科 - A historical figure, a famous hunter or law enforcement officer.
  • 廉颇 - A famous general from the Warring States period, known for his bravery and military prowess.
  • 山东 - Refers to the Shandong province, indicating a specific location.
  • 臬署 - Refers to the governor's office, indicating a governmental institution.

[211]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 疾如飞鸟而逝 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'swiftly like a flying bird,' emphasizing speed.
  • 老犹黑夜弹香火 - Literally 'still in old age, shooting in the dark with incense and fire,' likely a metaphor for practicing archery or shooting in low light conditions.
  • 援勍扣铁丸 - Literally 'seizing the strong and striking the iron ball,' a technical term for using a mechanical device to launch a projectile.
  • 诘旦 - Means 'the next day' or 'early morning.'
  • 逋逃薮 - Literally 'refuge for fugitives,' a metaphor for a place where criminals hide.
  • 额有伤者即盗 - Literally 'those with wounds on their forehead are thieves,' a specific criterion for identifying the thief.
  • 少纵即逝 - Means 'to let go a little and it will escape,' emphasizing the urgency of the situation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 漏 - Refers to the ancient Chinese water clock, used to measure time at night.
  • 香火 - Refers to incense and fire, often used in rituals and as a metaphor for lighting or illumination.
  • 稽臬署 - Refers to the office of the censor or the local government office responsible for law enforcement.
  • 抚军 - Refers to the governor or military commander, a high-ranking official in the imperial administration.
  • 捕役 - Refers to the local law enforcement officers or constables.

Proper Nouns

  • 梁 - The name of the archer.
  • 历城令 - The magistrate of Licheng.
  • 抚军 - The governor or military commander.
  • 稽臬署 - The office of the censor or the local government office responsible for law enforcement.

[212]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 公可召按察 - Direct command for the governor to summon the Censorate, a high-ranking official responsible for inspection and supervision.
  • 急谕稽臬署人等 - An urgent order to the officials of the Censorate, emphasizing the importance of the matter.
  • 额有伤者即盗，盗复何遁？ - Rhetorical question suggesting that anyone with injuries is a thief and cannot escape.
  • 顾此宜速不宜缓，缓则少纵即逝矣！ - Warning that the matter requires immediate action; delay will lead to the thief's escape.
  • 此事关系甚重 - Indicates that the matter is of great importance.
  • 如按察有疾，公姑枉驾省视 - Polite request for the governor to visit the Censorate if he is ill, maintaining respect and humility.
  • 僚属一体，必不得已，即寝室亦可晤语 - Emphasizes that officials are equals and can meet even in private if necessary.
  • 抚军然之 - The governor agrees with the suggestion.
  • 比至臬署，褚辞以不敢屈驾 - The Censorate politely refuses the governor's visit, citing illness.
  • 抚军执意省视其疾，褚益固辞 - The governor insists on visiting, but the Censorate continues to refuse.
  • 抚军大疑 - The governor becomes suspicious.
  • 即自下舆径入其寝室 - The governor personally enters the Censorate's bedroom.
  • 褚蒙头呻吟，不敢露面 - The Censorate hides his face, pretending to be ill.
  • 抚军命左右搴被而逼视之 - The governor orders his attendants to lift the blanket to see the Censorate's face.
  • 见褚额坟起，缠以布帕，血涔涔外流，确系弹伤无疑也 - The governor sees that the Censorate has a bullet wound on his forehead, confirming his suspicion.
  • 抚军瞪目诧曰：“奇哉！大盗斯得，我始固料不及此也！” - The governor expresses surprise and satisfaction at catching the thief.
  • 反署，使人风褚速自录供待劾 - The governor returns to his office and orders the Censorate to confess and be impeached.
  • 褚料难掩饰，果据实直承为盗不讳 - The Censorate realizes he cannot hide the truth and confesses to being a thief.
  • 抚军特疏入告，勅即正法 - The governor reports to the emperor, who orders the thief to be executed.
  • 自褚伏诛后，盗踪顿戢 - After the Censorate is executed, criminal activities cease.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 按察 - The Censorate, a high-ranking official responsible for inspection and supervision.
  • 抚军 - The governor, a high-ranking military and administrative official.
  • 稽臬署 - The office of the Censorate, responsible for inspection and supervision.
  • 弹伤 - A bullet wound, indicating a violent injury.
  • 正法 - Execution, a severe punishment for serious crimes.
  • 盗踪顿戢 - Criminal activities cease, indicating a sudden end to lawlessness.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence is a complex command and rhetorical question, emphasizing the urgency and the inevitability of the thief's capture.
  • This sentence warns against delay, using a parallel structure to emphasize the consequences.
  • This sentence introduces the gravity of the matter, setting the tone for the rest of the passage.
  • This sentence suggests a polite request for the governor to visit the Censorate if he is ill, maintaining respect and humility.
  • This sentence emphasizes the equality of officials and the flexibility of their interactions.
  • This sentence shows the governor's agreement with the suggestion.
  • This sentence describes the Censorate's polite refusal of the governor's visit, citing illness.
  • This sentence shows the governor's persistence and the Censorate's continued refusal.
  • This sentence indicates the governor's growing suspicion.
  • This sentence describes the governor's decisive action in entering the Censorate's bedroom.
  • This sentence describes the Censorate's attempt to hide his face, pretending to be ill.
  • This sentence describes the governor's order to his attendants to lift the blanket to see the Censorate's face.
  • This sentence describes the governor's discovery of the Censorate's bullet wound, confirming his suspicion.
  • This sentence shows the governor's surprise and satisfaction at catching the thief.
  • This sentence describes the governor's return to his office and his order for the Censorate to confess and be impeached.
  • This sentence shows the Censorate's realization that he cannot hide the truth and his subsequent confession.
  • This sentence describes the governor's report to the emperor, who orders the thief to be executed.
  • This sentence describes the cessation of criminal activities after the Censorate's execution.

Proper Nouns

  • 按察 - The Censorate.
  • 抚军 - The governor.
  • 稽臬署 - The office of the Censorate.
  • 弹伤 - A bullet wound.
  • 正法 - Execution.
  • 盗踪顿戢 - Criminal activities cease.

[213]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 里乘子曰 - Classical opening formula meaning 'Li Chengzi said,' introducing a narrative or commentary.
  • 予曩在临清 - Temporal phrase meaning 'I once was in Linqing,' setting the scene for the narrator's recollection.
  • 谈褚事甚详 - Means 'talked about Chu's affairs in great detail,' indicating a thorough account.
  • 楷字摹褚登善 - Refers to the practice of copying Chu Danxiang's calligraphy, a renowned calligrapher.
  • 大草仿米元章 - Refers to imitating Mi Yuanzhang's cursive script, another famous calligrapher.
  • 入妙 - Means 'reaching the highest level of skill,' emphasizing exceptional proficiency.
  • 堂堂风宪 - Refers to a high-ranking official, specifically a censor or inspector.
  • 甘作盗魁 - Means 'willingly become a bandit leader,' indicating a shocking moral lapse.
  • 延颈受诛 - Means 'extend the neck to accept execution,' a vivid description of accepting punishment.
  • 无悔 - Means 'without regret,' highlighting the subject's stubbornness.
  • 不知是何居心也 - Means 'does not know what kind of heart he has,' expressing puzzlement at the subject's motives.
  • 美而好武 - Means 'beautiful and fond of martial arts,' describing the character's physical and personality traits.
  • 嫁同邑武进士某 - Means 'married a martial arts jinshi from the same county,' indicating a union with a distinguished military scholar.
  • 亦美男子也 - Means 'also a handsome man,' describing the husband.
  • 骑镂金鞍枣骝马 - Means 'ride a saddle adorned with gold on a chestnut bay horse,' detailing the character's appearance.
  • 惯着缁衣素裳 - Means 'used to wear black robes and plain skirts,' indicating a preference for simple attire.
  • 短襟秃袖 - Means 'short-sleeved and sleeveless,' describing the style of clothing.
  • 五色丝绦 - Means 'a multi-colored silk belt,' a decorative accessory.
  • 左悬朱弦雕弓 - Means 'hang a red-stringed carved bow on the left,' indicating a weapon.
  • 右悬锦箙 - Means 'hang a brocade quiver on the right,' indicating a container for arrows.
  • 内盛鹫翎金仆姑 - Means 'inside holds eagle-feathered golden arrows,' describing the contents of the quiver.
  • 两足蹑利屣 - Means 'both feet wear sharp shoes,' indicating a type of footwear.
  • 立镫上纤细如锥 - Means 'stand on the stirrups as thin as needles,' describing the character's posture and appearance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 褚登善 - A renowned calligrapher from the Tang Dynasty, known for his elegant and refined style.
  • 米元章 - Mi Fu, a famous calligrapher from the Song Dynasty, known for his bold and expressive cursive script.
  • 武进士 - A military scholar who passed the imperial examination, indicating a high level of martial and scholarly achievement.
  • 缁衣素裳 - Traditional Chinese clothing, with '缁衣' referring to black robes and '素裳' to plain skirts, often associated with simplicity and modesty.
  • 五色丝绦 - A multi-colored silk belt, often used as a decorative accessory in traditional Chinese attire.
  • 朱弦雕弓 - A red-stringed carved bow, a symbol of martial prowess and elegance.
  • 锦箙 - A brocade quiver, a decorative container for arrows.
  • 鹫翎金仆姑 - Eagle-feathered golden arrows, indicating high-quality and prestigious weapons.

Proper Nouns

  • 里乘子 - The narrator, Li Chengzi.
  • 褚登善 - A renowned calligrapher from the Tang Dynasty.
  • 米元章 - Mi Fu, a famous calligrapher from the Song Dynasty.
  • 武进士 - A military scholar who passed the imperial examination.
  • 毅华 - The daughter's name.
  • 果男 - The daughter's courtesy name.
  • 讷儿 - The daughter's nickname.
  • 临清 - A place name, possibly a county or city.

[214]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 頭上青帕抹額 - Literal description of a headband or headscarf worn on the forehead.
  • 發绾盘龙高髻黑如髹漆 - Describes a high coiled hairstyle like a dragon, black as lacquer, emphasizing its elegance and color.
  • 髻前插盏大红绒球二颗 - Refers to two large red wool balls inserted into the hair, adding a vibrant and ornate touch.
  • 眉弯新月目莹秋波 - Uses metaphors to describe the eyebrows as crescent-shaped and the eyes as bright and reflective like autumn waves.
  • 玉靥朱唇，不假涂泽 - Describes the cheeks as jade-like and the lips as red, implying natural beauty without makeup.
  • 并辔而驰，俨然双璧 - A metaphor comparing the couple to two precious jade discs, emphasizing their harmonious and beautiful appearance together.
  • 游击荐擢总戎 - Refers to the military promotion from a lower rank to a higher command position.
  • 每阅武，女必坐屏后观之 - Describes the practice of sitting behind a screen to observe military exercises.
  • 有好身手者辄另赏以银币 - Means that those with good skills receive additional silver rewards.
  • 麾下卧彪之俦 - Refers to the elite soldiers under her husband's command, likened to sleeping tigers.
  • 技勇非他部所能敌 - Emphasizes the exceptional skill and bravery of these soldiers.
  • 谙彭祖内视法 - Refers to the knowledge of a longevity technique attributed to Peng Zu, an ancient Chinese figure known for his long life.
  • 当随夫之任时年已逾四十，望之犹二十许人 - Describes her appearance as youthful despite being over forty years old.
  • 亦善书，尝见骨董客藏所临《十三行经》泥金便面 - Indicates that she is also skilled in calligraphy, and a collector once owned a copy of her work on a gold-leaf fan.
  • 某太史以朱提一流购得之，狂喜如获异宝云 - Describes a historian purchasing her work for a high price and being ecstatic about it.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 青帕抹额 - A traditional headband or headscarf, often worn by women in ancient China.
  • 盘龙高髻 - A high coiled hairstyle, often associated with elegance and beauty in classical Chinese culture.
  • 髹漆 - Lacquer, a material used for decorative purposes, indicating the high quality and value of the hair accessory.
  • 新月 - A crescent shape, often used to describe graceful and delicate features.
  • 秋波 - A poetic term for bright and reflective eyes, often used to describe beautiful women.
  • 玉靥 - Jade-like cheeks, a common metaphor for fair and smooth skin.
  • 朱唇 - Red lips, another common metaphor for beauty.
  • 并辔而驰 - Riding side by side, often used to describe a harmonious and elegant couple.
  • 双璧 - Two precious jade discs, a metaphor for two beautiful and harmonious individuals.
  • 游击 - A military rank, indicating a lower-level officer.
  • 总戎 - A high military rank, indicating a general or commander.
  • 阅武 - Military exercises or reviews, a common practice in ancient Chinese military culture.
  • 屏后 - Behind a screen, a place where spectators could observe without being seen.
  • 银币 - Silver coins, a form of currency in ancient China.
  • 麾下 - Under one's command, referring to the soldiers or subordinates.
  • 卧彪 - A metaphor for sleeping tigers, indicating elite and powerful soldiers.
  • 彭祖内视法 - A longevity technique attributed to Peng Zu, an ancient Chinese figure known for his long life.
  • 骨董客 - A collector or connoisseur of antiques.
  • 《十三行经》 - A classic text, possibly referring to a specific religious or philosophical work.
  • 泥金便面 - A gold-leaf fan, a luxurious and valuable item.
  • 太史 - A historian or scholar, often holding a high position in the imperial court.
  • 朱提一流 - A high price, indicating the value of the item.

Proper Nouns

  • 彭祖 - Peng Zu, an ancient Chinese figure known for his longevity.
  • 《十三行经》 - A classic text, possibly referring to a specific religious or philosophical work.
  • 泥金便面 - A gold-leaf fan, a luxurious and valuable item.
  • 太史 - A historian or scholar, often holding a high position in the imperial court.

[215]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 古雏鸾 - A poetic name for a young woman, likely a fox spirit, with '古' meaning ancient or old, and '雏鸾' referring to a young phoenix, symbolizing beauty and grace.
  • 容止都美 - A phrase meaning 'graceful demeanor and beautiful appearance,' describing someone's overall charm.
  • 大名太守 - Refers to the governor of Daming, with '大名' being a place name and '太守' meaning governor.
  • 司记室 - Refers to an official position, likely a secretary or recorder.
  • 搴帷入 - A poetic expression meaning 'to pull the curtain and enter,' describing a woman's graceful entrance.
  • 姿首娟妍 - A phrase meaning 'beautiful and delicate features,' describing a woman's appearance.
  • 目波明媚 - A poetic expression meaning 'eyes like waves of light,' describing a woman's captivating gaze.
  • 诚天人也 - A phrase meaning 'truly a heavenly being,' praising someone's beauty.
  • 妾狐仙也 - A phrase meaning 'I am a fox spirit,' with '妾' being a humble term for a woman, '狐仙' meaning fox spirit.
  • 古氏雏鸾 - A poetic name for the fox spirit, likely referring to her family or origin.
  • 与君有缘 - A phrase meaning 'fated to be with you,' indicating a destined connection.
  • 非祸君者 - A phrase meaning 'not here to harm you,' assuring the man of her intentions.
  • 可请毋畏 - A phrase meaning 'please do not be afraid,' urging the man to relax.
  • 遂不复畏 - A phrase meaning 'and no longer feared,' indicating the man's change in attitude.
  • 与同寝处 - A phrase meaning 'to share the same bed and room,' indicating a close relationship.
  • 情好甚敦 - A phrase meaning 'deep affection and closeness,' describing the strong bond between them.
  • 女畅通文义 - A phrase meaning 'the woman is well-versed in literature and rhetoric,' praising her intellectual abilities.
  • 兼擅书法 - A phrase meaning 'also skilled in calligraphy,' indicating her artistic talents.
  • 遇生事烦，辄为捉刀 - A phrase meaning 'when the man is busy, she often writes for him,' describing her practical support.
  • 字体秀健 - A phrase meaning 'her handwriting is elegant and robust,' praising her calligraphy.
  • 出生之右 - A phrase meaning 'superior to the man,' indicating her literary skill surpasses his.
  • 每戒生慎勿泄语 - A phrase meaning 'she often warns the man to keep their relationship secret,' indicating her concern for confidentiality.
  • 人渐有知者 - A phrase meaning 'people gradually begin to know,' indicating the relationship becomes public.
  • 太守起家翰林 - A phrase meaning 'the governor rose to the position of Hanlin Scholar,' indicating his high status.
  • 亦风雅士 - A phrase meaning 'also a refined and cultured man,' praising his literary and artistic tastes.
  • 私以问生 - A phrase meaning 'privately asked the man,' indicating a confidential inquiry.
  • 质言不敢稍讳 - A phrase meaning 'speaking frankly without any concealment,' indicating the man's honesty.
  • 并坚嘱勿以语人 - A phrase meaning 'and firmly warned not to tell others,' indicating his commitment to secrecy.
  • 如不泄语 - A phrase meaning 'if you do not reveal it,' indicating a condition for the governor's request.
  • 须君意中人私觌 - A phrase meaning 'you must introduce your beloved privately,' indicating the governor's request to meet the woman.
  • 俾得一亲芳姿 - A phrase meaning 'so that he can meet her beauty,' indicating the governor's desire to see the woman.
  • 乃如所戒 - A phrase meaning 'and follow your warning,' indicating the man's commitment to secrecy.
  • 退以语女 - A phrase meaning 'told the woman afterward,' indicating the man's communication with her.
  • 太守公不怀好意 - A phrase meaning 'the governor does not have good intentions,' indicating the woman's suspicion of the governor's motives.
  • 挟势劫盟 - A phrase meaning 'using his power to force an agreement,' indicating the governor's coercive behavior.
  • 妾必有以小惩之 - A phrase meaning 'I will surely find a way to punish him lightly,' indicating the woman's determination to respond to the governor's actions.
  • 生观书，拥女于怀 - A phrase meaning 'the man was reading, holding the woman in his arms,' describing a tender moment.
  • 太守缓步搴帷迅入 - A phrase meaning 'the governor slowly pulled the curtain and quickly entered,' describing his unexpected arrival.
  • 女翩若惊鸿 - A phrase meaning 'the woman gracefully rose like a startled swan,' describing her elegant movement.
  • 起身遽遁 - A phrase meaning 'quickly stood up and fled,' indicating her sudden departure.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 江左 - Refers to the region east of the Yangtze River, historically a center of culture and politics.
  • 奚生铁臣 - A proper noun referring to a specific person, likely a scholar or official, with '奚生' being a given name and '铁臣' being a courtesy name.
  • 大名太守 - Refers to the governor of Daming, with '大名' being a place name and '太守' meaning governor.
  • 司记室 - Refers to an official position, likely a secretary or recorder.
  • 狐仙 - Refers to a fox spirit, a common motif in Chinese folklore, often depicted as a beautiful and mischievous being.
  • 雏鸾 - A poetic name for a young woman, symbolizing beauty and grace.
  • 翰林 - Refers to a high-ranking scholar-official in the imperial court, often associated with literary and artistic talents.
  • 风雅士 - Refers to a refined and cultured man, often associated with literary and artistic pursuits.

Proper Nouns

  • 江左奚生铁臣 - A proper noun referring to a specific person, likely a scholar or official, with '江左' meaning the region east of the Yangtze River, '奚生' being a given name, and '铁臣' being a courtesy name.

[216]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 瞥睹其貌 - Literally 'glanced at her appearance,' used to describe a brief, admiring look.
  • 不禁神驰 - Means 'could not help but be entranced,' expressing a strong emotional reaction.
  • 戏抚生肩 - Means 'playfully patted the young man's shoulder,' indicating a friendly and affectionate gesture.
  • 君几生修此艳福 - Means 'How many lifetimes have you cultivated to enjoy such good fortune?' A rhetorical question emphasizing the rarity and value of the situation.
  • 佳人不媒而合 - Means 'a beautiful woman has come together without the need for a matchmaker,' highlighting the natural and spontaneous nature of the union.
  • 夙夜必偕 - Means 'will surely be together day and night,' expressing a commitment to a close relationship.
  • 鄙人一求平视而不可得 - Means 'I, a lowly person, cannot even get a level look from her,' indicating a sense of inadequacy or unattainability.
  • 能勿羡而生妒乎 - Means 'how can I not envy and feel jealous?' Expressing a natural human reaction to the situation.
  • 公以堂堂太守 - Means 'You, as a respectable governor,' addressing the official's high status.
  • 后房粉白黛绿 - Means 'in the inner chambers, with painted faces and green eyebrows,' describing the beauty of the women in the governor's household.
  • 争怜斗宠者不知凡几 - Means 'there are countless women competing for his favor,' emphasizing the number and intensity of the competition.
  • 尚不自慊 - Means 'still do not feel satisfied,' indicating a sense of dissatisfaction despite having much.
  • 而鳃鳃然歆羡于穷措大 - Means 'yet you are so envious of a poor scholar,' highlighting the contrast between the governor's wealth and the scholar's poverty.
  • 能毋愧乎 - Means 'how can you not feel ashamed?' Expressing a moral judgment.
  • 生以将应京兆试 - Means 'The young man, preparing to take the capital examination,' indicating the scholar's ambition and current activity.
  • 偶取时艺翻阅 - Means 'happened to pick up and read contemporary essays,' describing the scholar's casual action.
  • 君不知取法乎上 - Means 'You do not know to take the highest standards as your model,' criticizing the scholar for not aiming high.
  • 徒此孜孜 - Means 'just working hard like this,' indicating a lack of direction or ambition.
  • 纵终日咿唔 - Means 'even if you study all day long,' emphasizing the futility of the scholar's efforts.
  • 口角流沫 - Means 'spitting foam from your mouth,' a vivid description of intense study.
  • 何益也 - Means 'what good will it do?' Expressing skepticism about the value of the scholar's efforts.
  • 卿世外人 - Means 'You are an outsider to the world,' suggesting that the woman is not bound by conventional social norms.
  • 亦解此小道耶 - Means 'do you understand this trivial skill?' Criticizing the scholar for not recognizing the importance of the art of writing.
  • 时文为词章根本 - Means 'Contemporary essays are the foundation of literary composition,' emphasizing the importance of mastering the basics.
  • 时文不精，各体俱难畅达 - Means 'If you do not master contemporary essays, it will be difficult to excel in other forms of writing,' highlighting the foundational role of contemporary essays.
  • 每慨三家村伪儒 - Means 'I often lament the fake scholars in the villages,' expressing disappointment in the quality of local scholars.
  • 时文不通，辄自命以诗古文词名家 - Means 'who do not understand contemporary essays but still call themselves masters of poetry and ancient prose,' criticizing their pretentiousness.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太守 - Refers to the governor of a prefecture, a high-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 京兆试 - Refers to the imperial examination held in the capital, a crucial step in the civil service examination system.
  • 时艺 - Refers to contemporary essays, a genre of writing that was highly valued in the imperial examination system.
  • 三家村伪儒 - Refers to fake scholars in the villages, a derogatory term for those who claim to be learned but lack genuine knowledge.
  • 诗古文词 - Refers to poetry, ancient prose, and literary essays, genres that were highly respected in the classical Chinese literary tradition.

Proper Nouns

  • 太守 - The title of the governor.
  • 京兆试 - The name of the imperial examination.
  • 时艺 - The term for contemporary essays.
  • 三家村伪儒 - A derogatory term for fake scholars in the villages.
  • 诗古文词 - Terms for poetry, ancient prose, and literary essays.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一时贵耳贱目者 - Those who value hearsay over seeing, referring to people who judge based on reputation rather than actual merit.
  • 遽以名士相推 - Hastily elevate someone to the status of a distinguished scholar, indicating a quick and possibly unwarranted promotion.
  • 彼竟居之不疑 - He accepts it without doubt, suggesting a lack of critical thinking.
  • 自以为是 - To think oneself right, indicating arrogance or overconfidence.
  • 不愧学植无根 - Not ashamed of having no solid foundation in learning, suggesting a lack of genuine knowledge.
  • 反大言欺人 - Instead, speak grandly to deceive others, indicating hypocrisy.
  • 肆口丑诋时文为小道 - To openly insult contemporary prose as a minor pursuit, showing contempt.
  • 鄙弃不屑染指 - To despise and avoid touching it, indicating a strong aversion.
  • 而后生小子希慕虚声 - And young people aspire to empty fame, suggesting a pursuit of superficial recognition.
  • 乐从其道 - Enjoy following his path, indicating blind adherence.
  • 相率为伪 - To collectively act falsely, suggesting a widespread deception.
  • 文风既坏 - The literary style has already deteriorated, indicating a decline in quality.
  • 学术奚端 - Where is the foundation of scholarship? Expressing concern about the lack of solid academic principles.
  • 是则可忧也 - This is indeed a cause for worry, indicating a sense of alarm.
  • 代圣贤立言 - To speak on behalf of sages, suggesting a high moral and intellectual standard.
  • 镕经铸史 - To refine and shape through the classics and histories, indicating a deep engagement with traditional texts.
  • 醖酿而出 - To mature and emerge, suggesting a process of refinement and development.
  • 率尔操觚 - To hastily take up the brush, indicating a lack of preparation or care.
  • 草草毕事 - To finish carelessly, suggesting a lack of thoroughness.
  • 村伧不察 - Villagers who do not understand, indicating a lack of knowledge or insight.
  • 猥以小道目之 - To crudely label it as a minor pursuit, suggesting a dismissive attitude.
  • 不亦慎乎 - Is this not cautious? Indicating a rhetorical question that challenges the listener's judgment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 名士 - Distinguished scholars or intellectuals, often with a connotation of moral and intellectual superiority.
  • 时文 - Contemporary prose or essays, often used in the context of literary and academic writing.
  • 制艺 - The genre of essay writing used in imperial examinations, emphasizing its importance in the educational system.
  • 八股 - The specific format of the imperial examination essays, characterized by strict structure and content requirements.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sentence sets up the theme of judging based on reputation rather than merit.
  • This clause describes the behavior of those who accept the false reputation and act arrogantly, without genuine knowledge.
  • This clause explains how younger generations are influenced by the false reputation and follow the same path.
  • This clause summarizes the negative consequences of the false reputation on literary and academic standards.
  • This clause explains the proper method of writing contemporary prose, emphasizing the need for deep engagement with traditional texts.
  • This clause criticizes the villagers for their lack of understanding and dismissive attitude towards contemporary prose.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 成、宏、正、嘉，规模于焉大备 - The Zhengde, Hongzhi, Jiajing, and Wanli eras saw the comprehensive development of literary forms.
  • 其朴实说理处，犹不免近于高头讲章 - Their straightforward and rational parts are still close to the style of formal expositions.
  • 隆、万专尚神韵，多涉浅滑 - The Longqing and Wanli periods specialized in elegance and charm, often being superficial and slippery.
  • 天、崇则称极盛矣 - The Tianqi and Chongzhen periods are considered the peak.
  • 海阳、太乙、蕴生、大樽四家可称四杰 - The four families of Haiyang, Taiyi, Yunsheng, and Dazun can be called the Four Greats.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天、崇 - Refers to the Tianqi (1620-1627) and Chongzhen (1627-1644) eras.
  • 海阳、太乙、蕴生、大樽 - Refers to specific literary figures or families known for their contributions to Ming literature.
  • 仲昭 - Refers to a specific literary figure whose work is evaluated in the text.
  • 熊、刘、韩、马 - Refers to prominent literary figures of the current dynasty.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 既下第歸 - Having failed the examination and returned home.

  • 爰別太守，邀女偕行 - He bid farewell to the governor and invited the woman to travel with him.
  • 固嫌妻貌寝 - He had always disliked his wife's appearance.
  • 差可為葑菲之采 - Fairly acceptable as a patch of wild vegetables.
  • 殊不相配 - Completely mismatched.
  • 如卿肯偕歸，平生願足矣 - If you would accompany me, my lifelong wish would be fulfilled.
  • 巧女多伴拙夫 - Clever women often marry clumsy men.
  • 美男亦多娶丑女 - Handsome men also often marry ugly women.
  • 人多謂配合颠倒，为之不平 - Many people say that such pairings are upside down and feel it is unfair.
  • 不知天地爱人以德 - But they do not know that heaven and earth love people according to their virtue.
  • 造物妙用 - The ingenious use of creation.
  • 秦嘉徐淑 - Qin Jia and Xu Shu.
  • 相如文君 - Xiangru and Wenjun.
  • 伉俪相当 - Spouses are well-matched.
  • 青春相爱，白首难期 - They love each other in their youth, but it is hard to expect a long life together.
  • 琴瑟常调，弦多中断 - The harmony of the qin and se is often disrupted by broken strings.
  • 转不若梁孟、鲍桓，弃貌取德，反可山河偕老也 - But it is better than the cases of Liang Meng and Bao Huan, who valued virtue over appearance and thus lived together until old age.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秦嘉徐淑 - Qin Jia and Xu Shu were a couple known for their literary works and their mutual respect and love. Qin Jia was a poet, and Xu Shu was a writer.
  • 相如文君 - Zuo Xiangru and Zhuo Wenjun were a famous couple in Chinese history. Their love story is often cited as a model of romantic and literary partnership.
  • 梁孟 - Liang Hong and Meng Guang were a couple known for their mutual respect and devotion. Liang Hong was a scholar, and Meng Guang was a woman who refused to marry unless she could serve her husband as a servant.
  • 鲍桓 - Bao Shuya and his wife were known for their deep affection and commitment to each other, despite Bao Shuya's physical deformity.

Proper Nouns

  • 秦嘉 - Qin Jia, a poet.
  • 徐淑 - Xu Shu, a writer.
  • 相如 - Zuo Xiangru, a scholar and poet.
  • 文君 - Zhuo Wenjun, a writer and Zuo Xiangru's wife.
  • 梁孟 - Liang Hong, a scholar, and Meng Guang, his wife.
  • 鲍桓 - Bao Shuya, a man with a physical deformity, and his wife.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 至君嫌夫人貌寝，殊可不必 - Formal expression indicating it is unnecessary for a man to criticize his wife's appearance, especially if she is virtuous.
  • 妾知夫人命薄，不堪为孝廉之配 - Statement expressing that the speaker knows the wife has a short life and is not suitable to be the wife of a scholar-official.
  • 君归不久，必不免奉倩神伤 - Phrase suggesting that the husband will soon experience deep sorrow after returning home.
  • 旧人既逝，新人即来 - Statement indicating that after the death of the old wife, a new wife will come.
  • 君具此才貌，又得一第，何虑无嫱施作匹 - Rhetorical question implying that the husband, with his talents and appearance, and having achieved success, has no reason to worry about finding a suitable match.
  • 妾相君鸾胶重续，定属嘉耦 - Metaphorical expression suggesting that the speaker believes the husband will find a good match again.
  • 赠嫁甚丰 - Indicates that the new wife will be given a generous dowry.
  • 君命非仕宦中人，此后可戢景邱园，春晖奉母 - Suggests that the husband's fate is not to be a government official, and he should instead retire to the countryside to care for his mother.
  • 日对丽人，坐拥厚资，一袜同心，南面不易 - Describes a life of luxury and comfort, with a beautiful wife, wealth, and harmony.
  • 有妾在旁，反使目中添刺 - Statement indicating that the presence of the speaker would cause the husband discomfort or annoyance.
  • 况与君缘分垂尽，何如稍留未了，为他日重会计耶 - Rhetorical question suggesting that the speaker believes their relationship is almost over, and it would be better to leave some things unresolved for future reconciliation.
  • 生以女言决绝，料不可强，不得已，挥泪而别 - Describes the husband's decision to end the relationship based on the speaker's words, knowing he cannot force the situation, and leaving with tears.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 至君嫌夫人貌寝 - Refers to a husband criticizing his wife's appearance, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 妾知夫人命薄 - Reflects the belief in fate and destiny, common in Chinese culture.
  • 不堪为孝廉之配 - "孝廉" refers to a scholar-official, a position of high moral and intellectual standing in ancient China.
  • 奉倩神伤 - "奉倩" is a reference to a historical figure, Cai Wenji, known for her deep sorrow after the death of her husband.
  • 旧人既逝，新人即来 - A common theme in classical literature, reflecting the idea of moving on after loss.
  • 君具此才貌，又得一第 - "得一第" refers to achieving success in the imperial examinations, a key step in becoming a scholar-official.
  • 妾相君鸾胶重续 - "鸾胶" is a metaphor for reuniting after a separation, often used in love stories.
  • 赠嫁甚丰 - Refers to a generous dowry, a common practice in ancient Chinese marriages.
  • 君命非仕宦中人 - Suggests that the husband's fate is not to be a government official, reflecting the belief in predestination.
  • 戢景邱园 - Refers to retiring to the countryside, a common aspiration for scholars who have achieved success but wish to live a simpler life.
  • 春晖奉母 - A metaphor for filial piety, reflecting the importance of caring for one's parents in Chinese culture.
  • 日对丽人，坐拥厚资 - Describes a life of luxury and comfort, a common theme in classical literature.
  • 一袜同心 - A metaphor for harmony and unity in marriage.
  • 南面不易 - Refers to the difficulty of governing, often used to describe the challenges of being a ruler.
  • 有妾在旁，反使目中添刺 - A common theme in classical literature, reflecting the idea that the presence of a third party can cause discomfort.
  • 况与君缘分垂尽 - Reflects the belief in the inevitability of fate and the idea that relationships are predestined.
  • 生以女言决绝，料不可强，不得已，挥泪而别 - Describes a dramatic farewell, a common theme in classical literature.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentences are elliptical, with subjects and implied actions that need to be made explicit in English.

Proper Nouns

  • 孝廉 - A scholar-official, a position of high moral and intellectual standing in ancient China.
  • 奉倩 - A reference to Cai Wenji, a historical figure known for her deep sorrow after the death of her husband.
  • 鸾胶 - A metaphor for reuniting after a separation, often used in love stories.
  • 春晖 - A metaphor for filial piety, reflecting the importance of caring for one's parents in Chinese culture.
  • 南面 - Refers to the difficulty of governing, often used to describe the challenges of being a ruler.

  • 自是悯生穷困，不时助以膏火，阴有东床之意 - 自是悯生穷困 means 'from then on, he pitied the guest's poverty,' 不时助以膏火 means 'occasionally helped with expenses,' and 阴有东床之意 means 'had secret intentions for him as a son-in-law.' This phrase describes the host's growing interest in the guest.

  • 故每公欲周济生时，必怂恿而赞成之 - 故每公欲周济生时，必怂恿而赞成之 means 'therefore, whenever the host wanted to help the guest,姮儿 would encourage and support him.' This phrase describes姮儿's role in supporting the guest.
  • 公幼子年十三，方攻举业，苦无良师，欲延奚生课读 - 公幼子年十三，方攻举业 means 'the host's young son, aged thirteen, was studying for the imperial examinations,' 苦无良师 means 'lacked a good teacher,' and 欲延奚生课读 means 'wanted to invite the guest to tutor him.' This phrase describes the host's need for a tutor.

  • 我为汝择配不易 - A phrase indicating that the speaker has gone through great effort to find a suitable match for the listener.
  • 今幸许某公子 - A phrase expressing that the listener is now fortunate enough to be engaged to a certain young man.
  • 此邑中第一等大绅土 - Refers to the young man being the foremost gentry in the town.
  • 其父气焰炙手可热 - A metaphorical expression indicating that the young man's father has immense influence and power.
  • 朝廷向用方殷 - A phrase suggesting that the father is currently in high favor with the court.
  • 指日可望枚卜 - A phrase meaning that the young man can expect rapid advancement in his career.
  • 汝父老不长进 - A phrase criticizing the listener's father for not progressing in life.
  • 但图逸乐，遽而乞休 - Indicates that the listener's father only seeks pleasure and has recently retired.
  • 汝嫁去便督家政，一呼百诺 - A phrase describing the expected authority and obedience the listener will have in her new family.
  • 似此大富贵，何尚郁郁不乐耶 - A rhetorical question asking why the listener is still unhappy despite such great wealth and status.
  • 虽难舍我二老 - Indicates that the listener finds it difficult to leave her parents.
  • 幸在同邑，时可见面 - A phrase expressing that the listener can still visit her parents often since they live in the same town.
  • 盍为我言之 - An interrogative expression asking the listener to explain her concerns.
  • 姮儿不答 - Indicates that the listener remains silent.
  • 再三研诘，卒颦眉不发一语 - Describes the repeated questioning and the listener's final silence.
  • 夫人无奈，只得曲意谕慰而去 - Indicates that the lady is at a loss and can only offer some consolation before leaving.
  • 姮儿晨起较迟，尚未晓妆 - Describes the listener waking up late and not yet putting on makeup.
  • 侍儿为具早膳，悉却弗用 - Indicates that the maidservant prepared breakfast, but the listener refused to eat.
  • 蓬头对镜，脉脉若有所思 - Describes the listener sitting with disheveled hair, looking into the mirror, and appearing lost in thought.
  • 王媪适至，惊曰 - Indicates that the lady just arrived and was surprised by the listener's appearance.
  • 几日未见娘子，何忽清瘦若此 - A phrase expressing surprise at the listener's sudden thinness.
  • 我亦不解何忽若此，但觉此心毫无生人之乐 - A phrase indicating that the listener does not understand why she has become so thin and feels no joy in life.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 择配 - Refers to the practice of matchmaking in traditional Chinese society.
  • 绅土 - Refers to the gentry class, which was an important social group in traditional China.
  • 朝廷 - Refers to the imperial court, the central government of the emperor.
  • 乞休 - Refers to the act of retiring from official duties, often due to age or health.
  • 家政 - Refers to household management, which was an important role for women in traditional Chinese families.
  • 枚卜 - Refers to the process of selecting officials through a divination ritual, indicating rapid career advancement.
  • 逸乐 - Refers to seeking pleasure and comfort, often criticized in traditional Chinese moral discourse.
  • 清瘦 - A term often used to describe a person who is thin and pale, sometimes associated with illness or emotional distress.

Proper Nouns

  • 姮儿 - The name of the listener, a woman.
  • 夫人 - The lady, likely the mother or a close relative of the listener.
  • 王媪 - The name of the lady who just arrived.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 何患不有嘉耦 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Why should you worry about not finding a good match?' It implies that the person will easily find a suitable partner.
  • 妾自知薄命 - A statement meaning 'I know I have a short life.' It reflects the speaker's awareness of her own mortality.
  • 日来心绪恶劣，慵如中酒，病颇绵惙 - A description of the speaker's current state, meaning 'Recently, my mood has been terrible, I feel as lazy as if I were drunk, and I am quite ill.' It conveys a sense of physical and emotional distress.
  • 其不能久于人世也必矣 - A statement meaning 'It is certain that I cannot live long in this world.' It emphasizes the speaker's impending death.
  • 君幸努力自爱，好自为之 - A statement meaning 'You should take care of yourself and do well.' It is an encouragement to the listener to take responsibility for their own life.
  • 何必抵死与人争髑髅哉 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Why should you die fighting with others over a skull?' It suggests that the listener should not engage in pointless conflicts.
  • 繄龙门兮无枝，妃玉轸兮冰丝 - A poetic line meaning 'The dragon gate has no branches, the jade strings and ice silk are pure and refined.' It describes a beautiful and refined instrument.
  • 与子期兮静好，偕百年兮友之 - A poetic line meaning 'We promise to be together in peace, to be friends for a hundred years.' It expresses a commitment to a lasting and harmonious relationship.
  • 皇庆元年中秋，天水子昂为仲姬夫人铭于沤波馆 - A statement meaning 'In the first year of the Huangqing era during the Mid-Autumn Festival, Zishang of Tianshui inscribed this for Lady Zhongji at the Ouboguan.' It provides the date and the author of the inscription.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 何不求守牧 - Literally 'why not seek to become a governor?' Here, '守牧' refers to a local official or governor.
  • 固所愿也，奈资不足何 - 'It is indeed what I wish for, but what can be done about the lack of funds?' This phrase expresses a desire tempered by financial constraints.
  • 所绌几何 - 'How much is lacking?' This phrase inquires about the amount needed to fulfill a request or goal.
  • 五千金足矣 - 'Five thousand gold coins would be enough.' This specifies the amount required to achieve a goal.
  • 妾当足成之 - 'I will make up the difference.' This indicates the speaker's willingness to provide the necessary funds.
  • 甘就冷宦 - 'To willingly accept a cold official position.' This suggests accepting a less desirable or less prestigious job.
  • 筮日 - 'To choose a date by divination.' This practice was common in ancient China for important events.
  • 治任祖饯 - 'To prepare the journey and hold a farewell banquet.' This describes the preparations for a departure, including a farewell feast.
  • 趣速经营 - 'To hurry and manage the affairs.' This indicates the urgency to handle business quickly.
  • 早去早归，免妾久盼 - 'Go early and return early, so as not to make me wait too long.' This expresses the speaker's desire for the other person to return soon.
  • 唯唯 - 'Yes, yes' or 'Indeed.' This is a polite response indicating agreement.
  • 订期珍重而别 - 'To agree on a date and part ways with care.' This describes the act of agreeing to meet again and parting amicably.
  • 逾期，公子不至 - 'After the agreed date, the young man did not arrive.' This indicates that the young man failed to show up as planned.
  • 但饰语支吾 - 'To only use evasive and ambiguous language.' This describes a situation where someone avoids giving a clear answer.
  • 实告公子固携金遁归乡里 - 'To truthfully inform that the young man had taken the money and fled back to his hometown.' This reveals the young man's dishonesty.
  • 其妻悍妒，亦不敢纳妾媵 - 'His wife is fierce and jealous, and he dares not take concubines.' This explains why the young man could not take a concubine.
  • 笑谓甲曰 - 'Laughed and said to Mr. A.' This describes the speaker's reaction and the person she is speaking to.
  • 初见若言大气浮 - 'At first, I thought you were a man of great spirit and presence.' This reflects the speaker's initial impression.
  • 虑少年轻薄，不可终恃 - 'I was worried that you might be a young man who is frivolous and unreliable.' This expresses concern about the young man's character.
  • 今果然也 - 'Now it turns out to be true.' This confirms the speaker's earlier concerns.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 守牧 - Refers to local officials or governors in ancient China.
  • 筮日 - Divination to choose a date for important events, a common practice in ancient China.
  • 治任祖饯 - Preparing for a journey and holding a farewell banquet, reflecting traditional Chinese customs.
  • 冷宦 - A less desirable or less prestigious official position.
  • 订期珍重而别 - Agreeing on a date and parting amicably, a common practice in ancient Chinese social interactions.
  • 入京求官 - Seeking an official position in the capital, a common aspiration for ambitious individuals in ancient China.
  • 悍妒 - Describing a fierce and jealous wife, a common theme in ancient Chinese literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 公子 - Refers to a young man of noble birth.
  • 妾 - Refers to a woman speaking about herself in a humble manner.
  • 甲 - Refers to a man named Mr. A.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 業夫妻矣 - Colloquial phrase meaning 'we are already married' or 'our marriage is settled.'

  • 得少弋獲 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to gain a small harvest,' indicating modest success.

  • 籍可運籌生色 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to use it to plan and create,' indicating the potential for wealth to be used productively.
  • 一洗寒酸 - Phrase meaning 'to wash away poverty,' indicating the desire to overcome economic hardship.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夫妻 - Refers to a married couple, a fundamental social unit in Chinese culture.

  • 君 - Refers to the husband or master, indicating a higher status in the household hierarchy.

  • 自奉卮以獻 - Refers to raising a cup to offer a toast, a gesture of respect or gratitude.

  • 姑妄言之 - Refers to speaking carelessly, indicating a lack of seriousness or commitment.

  • 一洗寒酸 - Refers to washing away poverty, indicating the desire to overcome economic hardship.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '業夫妻矣，曷不早言' is a rhetorical question meaning 'we are already married, why didn’t you say it earlier?' The ambiguity lies in the timing and the implied expectation.
  • The phrase '妾物即君物，但揮霍耳，何假為' is a series of linked statements: 'what belongs to me now belongs to you, it’s just a matter of spending it, why bother?' The ambiguity lies in the casual attitude towards wealth.
  • The phrase '遂傾箱罄出所蓄十万金付之' is a vivid description of the action: 'she emptied her entire box and handed over ten thousand gold.' The ambiguity lies in the completeness of the action and the significance of the amount.
  • The phrase '罷官業鹾，不三年得子金三十万' is a sequence of actions: 'he retired from office and took up the salt trade, and within three years, he gained thirty thousand gold.' The ambiguity lies in the rapid accumulation of wealth.
  • The phrase '即罢所业，肆筵设席，延女上座，自奉卮以献曰' is a series of actions: 'he then stopped his business, set up a banquet, invited her to sit in the seat of honor, and raised his own cup to offer.' The ambiguity lies in the sequence of events and the significance of each action.
  • The phrase '赖卿母金，得少弋获，子孙不忧冻馁，皆卿之赐' is a series of statements: 'thanks to your mother’s wealth, I gained a small harvest, our descendants will not suffer from cold and hunger, all of this is your gift.' The ambiguity lies in the gratitude and the implications for future generations.
  • The phrase '虽然，卿出身平康，无不知者，仆纵疏狂，亦不合俨然聘为继配' is a series of statements: 'however, you come from a place of comfort, everyone knows about it, I may be careless, but it’s not right to formally marry you as a second wife.' The ambiguity lies in the contrast between the woman’s background and the man’s reservations.
  • The phrase '即仆自愿之，其如天下后世口实何' is a series of statements: 'even if I voluntarily do it, what will people in the future say?' The ambiguity lies in the concern for public opinion and future judgment.
  • The phrase '妾从君生子已扶床矣，何忽出此言' is a series of statements: 'I have already given birth to a child who can walk, why suddenly say this?' The ambiguity lies in the timing and the unexpected nature of the question.
  • The phrase '岂畴昔申旦之誓非君意耶' is a rhetorical question: 'isn’t the oath we made last night not your intention?' The ambiguity lies in the reference to a past agreement and the question of its validity.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 今幸如愿 - Means 'now that the wish has been fulfilled' or 'now that the desire has been realized.'
  • 卿之母金当仍归赵 - A phrase that indicates returning the mother's dowry to her family, with '金' referring to the dowry and '赵' likely being a family name.
  • 酬以什一之息 - Means to repay with a tenth of the interest, where '什一' refers to one-tenth.
  • 我有旨蓄，亦以御冬 - A metaphorical expression indicating that the speaker has stored provisions for the winter, '旨蓄' meaning 'delicious stores' or 'provisions.'
  • 老夫髦矣 - A self-deprecating expression meaning 'I am an old man now,' with '髦' referring to old age.
  • 卿近中年，独处鳏居 - Indicates that the person addressed is nearing middle age and is currently unmarried or widowed.
  • 两足存活 - A colloquial expression meaning 'to survive with both feet,' implying being alive and well.
  • 请永与卿诀矣 - A formal expression of farewell, meaning 'please allow me to bid you farewell forever.'
  • 诀则诀矣！妾所生雏将焉置之？ - The first part is a repetition of the farewell, and the second part asks about the placement of the child, '雏' referring to the child.
  • 卿如难割爱，将雏俱去可耳 - A conditional statement suggesting that if it is difficult to part with the child, then taking the child along is acceptable.
  • 乃鸠工庀材，大治第宅 - Means to gather workers and materials to renovate or build a grand house, '鸠工庀材' being a classical expression for gathering resources.
  • 购良田沃产 - Means to purchase fertile land and productive property.
  • 择老成纪纲司之 - Means to select experienced and reliable individuals to manage the affairs.
  • 每岁出纳，躬自会计 - Means to personally oversee the annual income and expenses, '躬自' emphasizing personal involvement.
  • 日益饶富 - Means to become increasingly wealthy.
  • 不惜厚俸，聘延名师，以课其子 - Means to generously provide salaries to attract renowned teachers to educate the child.
  • 子十四岁，周殁，女赍重赙，携子斩齐临吊 - Indicates that when the child was fourteen years old, Zhou died, and the woman brought heavy mourning gifts and accompanied the child in formal mourning attire to pay respects.
  • 周之二子拒之，不许入门，恸哭而返 - Means that Zhou's two sons refused to let them enter, and they returned home in tears.
  • 或谓女十岁时所为诗终成谶语，所谓心高命薄者非耶！ - A comment suggesting that the woman's poem written at ten years old might have foretold her fate, with '心高命薄' meaning 'ambitious but short-lived.'
  • 自以郁郁不乐，四十岁后改号瘦菊老人，然风骨珊珊，虽当中年，望之犹如二十许人 - Indicates that she felt unhappy and changed her name to 'Old Man of Lean Chrysanthemum' after forty, but despite this, she maintained a graceful demeanor and appeared youthful even in middle age.

  • 一无所事 - Means to have nothing to do, indicating a state of idleness.
  • 始仰屋默数堂皇自西讫东木椽若干 - Specific action of counting the wooden beams in the hall from west to east.

  • 计惟暂置妻孥，孑身入都向亲友称贷，再事区画 - Means the plan was to temporarily leave his family and go alone to the capital to borrow money from friends, then make further plans.
  • 故事旗员因公去官，例许请觐 - Means according to the old rules, flag officers who leave their posts for official reasons are allowed to request an audience.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 往予读《国策》、《汉书》 - '往予' means 'in the past, I'; '读' means 'read'; '《国策》、《汉书》' are the titles of two historical texts (Diplomatic Strategies and the Records of the Grand Historian).
  • 睹苏季子、朱翁子已事 - '睹' means 'to see' or 'to observe'; '苏季子、朱翁子' are historical figures (Sun Qinzi and Zhu Wengzi); '已事' means 'past events.'
  • 未尝不感慨太息 - '未尝不' means 'never fails to'; '感慨太息' means 'feel moved and sigh.'
  • 今闻相国所云 - '今' means 'now'; '闻' means 'to hear'; '相国' is a title for a high-ranking official; '所云' means 'what is said.'
  • 然是鼎有足矣 - '然' means 'thus' or 'so'; '是' means 'this'; '鼎' means 'tripod' or '鼎' as a metaphor for a complete set of things; '有足矣' means 'is sufficient' or 'is enough.'
  • 人生世上 - A common phrase meaning 'in life on earth.'
  • 势利富贵 - Refers to wealth, power, and status.
  • 盖可忽乎 - '盖' is an adverb meaning 'certainly' or 'indeed'; '可忽乎' means 'can be neglected' or 'can be disregarded.'
  • 圆光二则 - '圆光' refers to a type of spiritual or supernatural phenomenon; '二则' means 'two cases' or 'two stories.'
  • 梅生，四川人 - '梅生' is a name; '四川人' means 'a person from Sichuan.'

  • 尝馆六安州幕中 - '尝' means 'once'; '馆' means 'stay' or 'reside'; '六安州幕中' means 'in the office of Liu'an Prefecture.'
  • 会署有术士 - '会' means 'happened to'; '署' means 'office'; '有术士' means 'there was a magician or fortune-teller.'
  • 愿为作法 - '愿' means 'willing to'; '为作法' means 'to perform a ritual.'
  • 先令尽量酣饮而卧 - '先令' means 'firstly ordered'; '尽量' means 'to drink to one's heart's content'; '酣饮' means 'drunk'; '而卧' means 'and lie down.'
  • 戒众勿惊 - '戒' means 'warn'; '众' means 'everyone'; '勿惊' means 'do not be alarmed.'
  • 自坐生榻前 - '自坐' means 'sat down'; '生榻前' means 'in front of the bed of the subject.'

  • 口中喃喃诵咒 - '口中' means 'in the mouth'; '喃喃' means 'muttering'; '诵咒' means 'reciting spells.'
  • 呼十二岁识字童子谛视之 - '呼' means 'call'; '十二岁' means 'twelve years old'; '识字' means 'able to read'; '童子' means 'child'; '谛视之' means 'look closely at it.'
  • 少选 - Means 'a short while later.'
  • 掌中放光，圆明如镜矣 - '掌中放光' means 'light emitted from the palm'; '圆明如镜' means 'round and bright like a mirror.'
  • 镜中现馆舍，梅生卧榻上矣 - '镜中现馆舍' means 'the mirror shows the office'; '梅生卧榻上' means 'Mei Sheng lying on the bed.'
  • 梅生兴矣，出门矣，方渡于水之涯矣，旋陟于山之巅矣，升峻岭矣，履坦途矣，抵屋一所，登门矣，升堂矣，入室矣 - Describes a series of actions: '梅生兴矣' (Mei Sheng rises), '出门矣' (goes out), '方渡于水之涯矣' (crosses the water), '旋陟于山之巅矣' (climbs to the top of the mountain), '升峻岭矣' (ascends a steep hill), '履坦途矣' (walks on a flat path), '抵屋一所' (reaches a house), '登门矣' (enters the door), '升堂矣' (enters the hall), '入室矣' (enters the room).

  • 室中一少妇，凭几握管作书，梅生竟笑倚其傍，且拊其鬟而玩其字矣 - '室中一少妇' means 'a young woman in the room'; '凭几握管作书' means 'leaning on a table and writing with a brush'; '梅生竟笑倚其傍' means 'Mei Sheng smiles and leans against her side'; '且拊其鬟而玩其字矣' means 'and caresses her hair while admiring her writing.'

  • 噀之 - '喷之' (xun zhi) means 'sprayed it,' referring to the act of spraying water on the paper horse.
  • 佳人未许轻分别 - '佳人' (jia ren) means 'beautiful person' or 'lover,' and '未许轻分别' (wei xu qing fenbie) means 'does not allow us to part lightly,' indicating a reluctance to separate.
  • 寤 - '寤' (wu) means 'awakened' or 'woke up,' marking the end of the dream or magical experience.
  • 银鬃绿耳 - Descriptive terms for the horse, '银鬃' (yin zong) means 'silver mane,' and '绿耳' (lv er) means 'green ears,' suggesting a horse with distinctive features.

  • 食旧德，拥厚资 - Suggests that the family has inherited wealth and social standing.
  • 馀桃断袖 - Refers to two famous historical anecdotes about male homosexuality, one involving Emperor Ai of Han and another involving King Zhao of Chu.
  • 少美仪容 - Describes the young man's physical attractiveness.
  • 生性徇通 - Highlights his adaptable and perceptive nature.
  • 喜涉猎图史 - Indicates his broad interest in reading and learning.
  • 夜光珠 - Refers to a rare and valuable pearl, often associated with wealth and status.
  • 缀于角巾 - Describes the pearl being attached to a headband, a common accessory for scholars and nobles.
  • 不肯轻以示人 - Suggests the pearl's value and the owner's desire to keep it secret.
  • 会残暑初退 - Indicates a time of transition, when the intense summer heat has just subsided.
  • 夜脱巾庋斋中案头 - Describes the act of removing the headband and placing it on the desk in the study at night.
  • 晨兴，求之已亡 - Indicates the pearl's disappearance, suggesting a mysterious or supernatural event.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 倘合浦不还，则蚁命难活 - Literally means 'If the pearl is not returned, then the ant's life will be in danger.' The phrase '蚁命难活' is an idiomatic expression meaning 'the ant's life will be in danger.'
  • 素谙圆光之技 - Literally means 'He was well-versed in the technique of divination.' The phrase '谙圆光之技' is an idiomatic way of saying 'well-versed in divination.'
  • 特造俞告其事 - Literally means 'He specially went to see Yu and told him about the incident.' The phrase '特造俞告其事' is an idiomatic way of saying 'specially went to see Yu and told him about the incident.'
  • 君术妙圆澄，曷不一试 - Literally means 'Your technique is very precise and clear, why not try it once?' The phrase '曷不一试' is an idiomatic way of saying 'why not try it once?'
  • 我非为公子失珠，所愿是非立见，庶免波及无辜 - Literally means 'I am not here for the loss of the pearl, but I wish to see the truth immediately, to avoid innocent people being implicated.' The phrase '庶免波及无辜' is an idiomatic way of saying 'to avoid innocent people being implicated.'
  • 果是两童则已，否则，亦可为辨其诬，俾性命得全，功胜合尖浮图也 - Literally means 'If it is indeed the two boys, then that is it. Otherwise, it can also clear their false accusation, allowing them to live, which would be a great deed.' The phrase '功胜合尖浮图也' is an idiomatic way of saying 'a great deed.'
  • 俞固好善，恻然心动，乃诺之 - Literally means 'Yu, who was good and kind, was moved by compassion and agreed.' The phrase '恻然心动' is an idiomatic way of saying 'moved by compassion.'
  • 礼延俞至，命备黄纸十二张 - Literally means 'He invited Yu respectfully, and ordered the preparation of twelve yellow sheets of paper.' The phrase '礼延俞至' is an idiomatic way of saying 'invited Yu respectfully.'
  • 骈指作剑诀，书勅勒其上 - Literally means 'He clasped his fingers together like a sword, and wrote the command on it.' The phrase '骈指作剑诀' is an idiomatic way of saying 'clasped his fingers together like a sword.'
  • 口默诵咒，焚投地上 - Literally means 'He silently recited a spell and burned it on the ground.' The phrase '口默诵咒' is an idiomatic way of saying 'silently recited a spell.'
  • 选十二岁以内童子十馀人，环视之 - Literally means 'He selected more than ten children under twelve years old and had them circle around it.' The phrase '环视之' is an idiomatic way of saying 'had them circle around it.'

  • 能以奇零贷我否 - Means 'can you lend me a small amount of it'; asks if the person can lend a small amount of the large sum they have.
  • 蹙然 - Means 'with a frown' or 'looking troubled'; indicates the emotional state of the person speaking, showing concern or worry.
  • 小子守命安贫 - Means 'I have always been content with my fate and lived a simple life'; a defense of the person's lifestyle and reputation.
  • 君瞒他人可，不必瞒我 - Means 'You can deceive others, but there's no need to deceive me'; a reassurance that the speaker trusts the other person and does not need to hide anything.

  • 尚何虞诈为也 - Means 'Why should I worry about deceit?'; a rhetorical question expressing the speaker's confidence in the other person.

  • 如获利，彼此均之 - Means 'if we make a profit, we will share it equally'; outlines the terms of the agreement.

  • 冰人 - A matchmaker, a traditional figure in Chinese society responsible for arranging marriages.
  • 采舆 - A wedding carriage, a symbol of the bride's journey to her new home.

  • 及笄 - A traditional rite of passage for girls, marking their transition into adulthood and eligibility for marriage.
  • 嫂甚厌恶之 - The elder sister-in-law's negative attitude toward the daughter reflects the complex dynamics of family relationships in traditional Chinese society, where older relatives often played a significant role in the upbringing and behavior of younger members.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 盖随某乙来此 - 盖是引入话题的语气词，随某乙来此意为跟着某乙来到这里。

  • 翁佯为大喜 - 翁佯为大喜描述老人假装非常高兴。
  • 俄顷乙至 - 俄顷乙至表示不久之后，乙就到了。

  • 居然称翁婿焉 - 居然称翁婿焉表示出乎意料地称呼对方为翁婿关系。

  • 绐以早结 - 绐以早结意为欺骗说事情早就解决了。
  • 农家予已别娶多年，今抱子矣 - 农家予已别娶多年，今抱子矣说明农民的妻子已经改嫁多年，现在有了孩子。
  • 翁便讽女宜偕乙归里 - 翁便讽女宜偕乙归里意味着老人暗示女儿应该和乙一起回到故乡。
  • 女谋于乙 - 女谋于乙表示女儿与乙商量。
  • 乙以为无事 - 乙以为无事指乙认为没有问题。
  • 遂治装偕女归 - 遂治装偕女归意味着于是准备行李，带着女儿回家。
  • 翁既到家，即密诣县上状 - 翁既到家，即密诣县上状表示老人回到家后，秘密地去县里报案。
  • 遣隶拘乙至 - 遣隶拘乙至意味着派遣差役逮捕乙。
  • 讯得巅末 - 讯得巅末表示审问后得知了事情的始末。
  • 其案乃结 - 其案乃结意味着案件最终得到了解决。
  • 先是，爱儿夜窜时，雪迷失路，堕眢井中，呼救 - 先是，爱儿夜窜时，雪迷失路，堕眢井中，呼救描述了孩子夜晚逃跑时，在雪地里迷路，掉进了枯井里并呼救的情况。
  • 某寺僧晨出募斋，闻知女子，大喜，正将缒绳下拽 - 某寺僧晨出募斋，闻知女子，大喜，正将缒绳下拽描述了一个寺庙的和尚早晨外出化缘，听说有女子落井，非常高兴，正准备放下绳子救人。
  • 某乙故里中无赖，夜博方毕，过此见之，遂与僧同拽起 - 某乙故里中无赖，夜博方毕，过此见之，遂与僧同拽起描述了乙是一个无赖，晚上赌博结束后路过这里，看到和尚正在救女子，于是和和尚一起把她拉了上来。
  • 悦女之色，欲挟以私奔，虑僧败露，乘其不意，取扁杖当头力劈，僧痛楚仆地，乃拖入井中 - 悦女之色，欲挟以私奔，虑僧败露，乘其不意，取扁杖当头力劈，僧痛楚仆地，乃拖入井中描述了乙因为喜欢女子的容貌，想要挟持她私奔，担心和尚会泄露秘密，趁和尚不注意，用扁担击打他的头部，导致和尚痛苦地倒在地上，然后将他拖入井中。
  • 然后以言胁女，偕遁至河南，竟成夫妇 - 然后以言胁女，偕遁至河南，竟成夫妇描述了乙随后用言语威胁女子，两人一起逃到了河南，最终结为夫妻。
  • 官乃断以乙抵僧罪，爱儿仍归原夫 - 官乃断以乙抵僧罪，爱儿仍归原夫表示官员判决乙承担和尚的罪责，而爱儿仍然回到了原来的丈夫身边。
  • 以嫂氏谑语起衅，令批其颊以示薄惩 - 以嫂氏谑语起衅，令批其颊以示薄惩描述了因为嫂子的玩笑话引发了争端，官员命令打了她的脸颊作为轻微的惩罚。
  • 人皆称快 - 人皆称快表示大家都感到满意。
  • 厥后，嫂氏两颊因挞成创，终身脓腐，臭不可迩 - 厥后，嫂氏两颊因挞成创，终身脓腐，臭不可迩描述了之后，嫂子的脸颊因为被打形成了伤口，终身感染化脓，散发出难闻的气味。
  • 邻里鄙其为人，都置不齿 - 邻里鄙其为人，都置不齿表示邻居们都鄙视她的为人，都不与她来往。
  • 爱儿既仍归农家子，夫妇重聚，皆知为嫂氏所骗，伉俪倍笃 - 爱儿既仍归农家子，夫妇重聚，皆知为嫂氏所骗，伉俪倍笃描述了爱儿回到原来的丈夫身边，夫妇重新团聚，大家都知道是被嫂子欺骗了，夫妻之间的感情更加深厚。
  • 由此衔嫂入骨，毕世不与通庆吊 - 由此衔嫂入骨，毕世不与通庆吊表示从此对嫂子怀恨在心，终生不再与她交往，无论是喜事还是丧事都不相往来。

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 里乘子曰 - Introduction of a wise or authoritative voice in the village.
  • 妇人群居，谑浪笑傲 - Describes women gathered together, joking and laughing boisterously, a common literary trope for women's gatherings.
  • 殊足滋事 - Means 'sufficiently causes trouble,' suggesting that such behavior often leads to problems.
  • 更难保其无意外之患 - Means 'even more difficult to ensure there are no unexpected dangers,' emphasizing the risks of such behavior.
  • 爱儿之狱 - Refers to a legal case involving a beloved child.
  • 实由嫂氏恶谑阶厉 - Means 'the case was actually due to the wicked teasing of the sister-in-law,' indicating the root cause of the problem.
  • 轇轕五年不能剖决 - Means 'the case was entangled for five years without resolution,' suggesting prolonged legal difficulties.
  • 赖族子无心路遇，报翁赚归 - Means 'thanks to an unthinking encounter with a cousin, the grandfather was tricked back home,' describing an unexpected turn of events.
  • 杀人者抵，疑狱顿雪 - Means 'the killer was punished, and the suspicious case was suddenly cleared,' indicating a resolution of the case.
  • 可称快事 - Means 'it can be called a pleasant event,' expressing satisfaction with the outcome.
  • 迨至旧弦重续，琴瑟永调 - Means 'until the old strings were renewed, and the qin and se were always in harmony,' symbolizing reconciliation and harmony.
  • 追忆畴昔恶谑之言，至是始悟其妄 - Means 'reflecting on past wicked teasing, only now did they realize it was false,' indicating a realization of the consequences.
  • 衔嫂入骨，永绝庆吊 - Means 'holding a grudge against the sister-in-law to the bone, forever severing all greetings and condolences,' expressing deep resentment.
  • 固其宜也 - Means 'it is indeed appropriate,' justifying the actions.
  • 观嫂氏批颊成创，孽报显然 - Means 'observing the sister-in-law's cheek being slapped, the retribution was clearly evident,' indicating a visible consequence.
  • 生虽已惨受官刑，窃恐死犹不免下拔舌地狱也 - Means 'though already suffering a cruel punishment, secretly fearing that in death one might still not escape the hell of being pulled from the tongue,' suggesting ongoing karmic consequences.
  • 闺阁闻之，可不互相警戒欤 - Means 'women should not fail to warn each other,' urging caution and mutual vigilance.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 里乘子曰 - Formal way of introducing a statement, similar to 'It is said' or 'One says.'
  • 厨媪所为，悖谬已极 - '厨媪' refers to a female cook or kitchen servant. '悖谬已极' means 'utterly absurd' or 'completely erroneous.'
  • 雷所以不遽击之者，恕其懵懂无知耳 - '雷所以不遽击之者' means 'the reason why the thunder did not immediately strike her,' and '恕其懵懂无知耳' means 'is out of mercy for her ignorance and foolishness.'
  • 敛钱为铜丸 - '敛钱' means 'to collect money,' and '为铜丸' means 'into copper balls.' The character '' is a variant of '镕' (to melt), but its exact meaning here is unclear.
  • 惟西席某先生一分二百文绳穿无恙 - '西席' refers to a teacher, and '一分二百文绳穿无恙' means 'only one hundred and twenty coins were strung together without any issue.'
  • 其他阿堵物来历可想 - '阿堵物' is a term for money or valuables, and '来历可想' means 'its origin can be imagined.'

  • 甲虑无以款客，家惟蓄一牝鸡 - '甲' refers to the thief, '虑无以款客' means 'worried about not having enough to entertain guests,' and '家惟蓄一牝鸡' means 'his home only had a hen.'
  • 因与妻谋，诘旦将伸一脔之敬，时抱卵方鷧 - '因与妻谋' means 'so he planned with his wife,' '诘旦' means 'the next day,' '将伸一脔之敬' means 'to offer a small portion of respect,' and '时抱卵方鷧' means 'at that time, the hen was incubating eggs.'

  • 吾命明日合休，素受主人豢养恩，鼎镬所不敢辞 - '吾命明日合休' means 'my fate is to die tomorrow,' '素受主人豢养恩' means 'I have always received the master's kindness,' and '鼎镬所不敢辞' means 'I would not dare to avoid the cauldron and pot' (a metaphor for death).

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 少年果受刀，再拜，自刭而死 - Sequence of actions is clear but emotionally charged.
  • 老者起立，顾尸长叹，拾刀拭血，弢而佩之，又自取褥覆马背，牵出门外，乘之鞭驰而去 - Series of actions describing the old man's behavior after the young man's death.
  • 某闻之历历，初颇愕悸，以老者所言居然盗不离道，不禁点首称叹 - Narrator's reaction described in a series of emotional states.
  • 月色将中，不敢再寝，将起赶路，突闻东厢噭然有声，厉如老枭，某大惊，姑再窥之 - Sequence of events builds tension and suspense.
  • 俄，东厢房门启，一物自内出，长丈许，遍体白毛蒙茸，走至院中，东向望月，目光睒睗，烱若挚电 - Vivid and detailed description of a creature, creating a sense of awe and fear.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sequence describes a series of gruesome actions, each action building upon the previous one.
  • This sequence describes a surreal and disturbing dance, contrasting with the previous violent actions.

  • This sequence describes the person's reaction to the events and their hasty departure.
  • This long sequence describes the person's internal struggle, decision-making process, and final escape.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '予乃探名山，济巨川，陆不车，水不船' describes a series of actions, emphasizing the extraordinary nature of the narrator's travels.
  • The parallel structure '下达九幽之地，上升九重之天' emphasizes the extreme realms the narrator has visited.
  • The long sequence '十洲三岛，宇内所称名胜者，无不悉恣予之游观' lists various places and indicates the narrator's unrestricted exploration.
  • The sequence '不饥不寒，随遇而安，凭虚御风，居然散仙' describes the narrator's carefree and ethereal lifestyle.
  • The sequence '欲心未断，见美色不能忘情' describes the narrator's lingering desires and emotional attachments.
  • The sequence '谨守皂隶教，其所戒三种人概不敢犯' describes the narrator's adherence to a set of rules and prohibitions.
  • The sequence '见良家好妇人，亦渐垂涎而染指焉' describes the narrator's growing temptation and actions.
  • The sequence '偶过浙东温州府，会城外爨弄，男女观者如堵，中一及笄女郎，色独殊众' describes a specific event and the narrator's observation of a particular individual.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '夙与周夫人有缘，知其将为村妇之代，欲救之而苦无法，君等何不谅予心也！' is a complex statement that combines a predestined relationship, a prediction, a desire to help, and a rhetorical question. It can be broken down into: '夙与周夫人有缘' (I have a predestined relationship with Lady Zhou), '知其将为村妇之代' (I know she will become a village woman), '欲救之而苦无法' (I want to save her but am desperately unable), and '君等何不谅予心也' (Why can't you understand my heart?).
  • The sentence '倘夫人产于夜分，万一可救，然不可必也。' is a conditional statement followed by a possibility and a caution: '倘夫人产于夜分' (If Lady Zhou gives birth at midnight), '万一可救' (There might be a chance to save her), and '然不可必也' (But it is not certain).
  • The sentence '惠氏以目止之，会邻生又扶鸾，惠氏又降坛曰：“久欲救周夫人，今幸得一策，君可为孝廉贺。”' describes a sequence of actions and a statement: '惠氏以目止之' (Huishi stopped it with a glance), '会邻生又扶鸾' (The neighbor again supported the spirit board), '惠氏又降坛曰' (Huishi descended from the altar and said), and '久欲救周夫人，今幸得一策，君可为孝廉贺' (I have long wanted to save Lady Zhou, now I have found a plan, you can congratulate the person with the title of孝廉).
  • The sentence '子姑待之。' is a simple advice to wait and see.
  • The sentence '一日，某氏方倚枕与惠氏闲话，见门外一蓬首女鬼招村妇去，某氏问：“何事？”惠氏以目止之，会邻生又扶鸾，惠氏又降坛曰：“久欲救周夫人，今幸得一策，君可为孝廉贺。”' is a detailed description of a sequence of events: '一日，某氏方倚枕与惠氏闲话' (One day, a certain person was leaning on a pillow and chatting with Huishi), '见门外一蓬首女鬼招村妇去' (They saw a ghost with unkempt hair summon a village woman), '某氏问：“何事？”' (The certain person asked, 'What is happening?'), '惠氏以目止之' (Huishi stopped it with a glance), '会邻生又扶鸾' (The neighbor again supported the spirit board), '惠氏又降坛曰' (Huishi descended from the altar and said), and '久欲救周夫人，今幸得一策，君可为孝廉贺' (I have long wanted to save Lady Zhou, now I have found a plan, you can congratulate the person with the title of孝廉).

  • 约先往取物送回 - Means 'planned to go first to take the valuables back,' where '约' means to plan.
  • 妻待至翌日薄暮 - Means 'his wife waited until the next evening,' where '翌日' means the next day, and '薄暮' means late evening.
  • 踪迹杳然 - Means 'he had disappeared without a trace,' where '杳然' implies complete disappearance.
  • 心殊疑虑 - Means 'she was deeply suspicious,' indicating the wife's growing concern.
  • 不得已，以告他三老求往视之 - Means 'with no choice, she told the other village elders to go and check on her,' where '不得已' means with no choice.
  • 果见某氏尸藏芦苇中 - Means 'they indeed found Ms. [Name]'s body hidden in the reeds,' where '果见' means indeed found.
  • 面目如生，怒容可掬 - Means 'her face looked as if she were alive, with a look of anger on her face,' where '可掬' means visible or obvious.
  • 甲跪其旁，抚之，则已僵矣 - Means 'Mr. [Name] knelt beside her, touched her, and found that she was already stiff,' where '僵矣' means already stiff or dead.
  • 急趋白某官 - Means 'they hurriedly reported this to the official,' where '趋' means to hurry.
  • 始共知某甲险恶 - Means 'everyone then realized how wicked Mr. [Name] was,' where '险恶' means wicked or evil.
  • 为鬼索命而毙 - Means 'he was killed by the spirit of Ms. [Name],' where '索命' means to take a life.
  • 爰舁某氏尸殡于舟 - Means 'they then carried Ms. [Name]'s body to the boat for burial,' where '舁' means to carry, and '殡' means to bury.
  • 即取白金五十镒并某氏金玉珠宝等物 - Means 'they then took the fifty taels of silver and the gold, jade, and precious jewels belonging to Ms. [Name],' where '即取' means to take immediately.
  • 分犒诸三老 - Means 'and distributed the reward among the village elders,' where '分犒' means to distribute as a reward.
  • 而某甲独不与焉 - Means 'but Mr. [Name] was not included,' indicating the boatman's exclusion from the reward.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 某官 - Refers to an unnamed official, a common way to refer to a person of high social status in Classical Chinese.
  • 爱妾 - Refers to a concubine, a secondary wife in traditional Chinese society.
  • 三老 - Refers to local village elders or leaders, who often played a role in community affairs.
  • 白金 - Refers to silver, a valuable metal in ancient China.
  • 镒 - An ancient unit of weight, equivalent to about 24 taels or 300 grams.
  • 跳脱 - A type of bracelet, often made of precious materials.
  • 簪钏首饰 - Refers to hairpins, bracelets, and other ornaments, symbols of wealth and status.
  • 金玉珠宝 - Refers to gold, jade, and precious jewels, indicating the concubine's significant wealth.
  • 篙师 - Refers to a boatman or steersman, a common occupation in ancient China.
  • 港汊 - Refers to a side channel or branch of a river, a common feature of waterways in ancient China.
  • 暮夜 - Refers to late night, a time often associated with secrecy or criminal activity in Classical Chinese literature.
  • 薄暮 - Refers to late evening, a time when people might be less vigilant.
  • 索命 - Refers to the act of taking a life, often associated with supernatural retribution in Chinese folklore.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 里乘子曰 - Title or name, likely referring to a narrator or commentator in the text.
  • 某甲得尸，气尚未绝 - Literally means 'someone found a corpse, the breath was not yet extinguished.' Describes a situation where a person is found alive but barely so.
  • 大喜 - Means 'great joy' or 'extremely pleased.'
  • 向使将去求赏 - A conditional phrase meaning 'if he had taken the body away to seek a reward.'
  • 百镒之外 - Refers to a significant amount of money, 'beyond a hundred yi' (an ancient unit of weight).
  • 即另求某氏金玉珠宝等物 - Means 'then he could have sought other valuable items from a certain person.'
  • 未尝不可 - Means 'it would not have been impossible' or 'it could have been done.'
  • 观后仍以各物分镐诸三老 - Means 'later, he still distributed the various items among the three elders of the village.'
  • 可知某官并非悭吝人也 - Means 'thus, it is known that the official was not a stingy person.'
  • 乃某甲贪念一转 - Means 'but someone's greedy thoughts changed.'
  • 自谓算无遗策 - Means 'he thought his plan was foolproof.'

  • 而己亦卒为鬼索命而毙 - Means 'and he himself was eventually killed by the ghost seeking revenge.'
  • 某官分犒诸三老，甲独不与 - Means 'the official distributed rewards among the three elders, but someone was excluded.'
  • 然则险恶之人，复何益哉 - Means 'thus, what good does a wicked person do?'
  • 某氏一弱女子耳 - Means 'someone was just a weak woman.'
  • 使非其鬼有灵 - Means 'if it were not for the spirit of the ghost.'
  • 几致沉冤莫白 - Means 'almost led to a deep injustice remaining unresolved.'
  • 观其面目如生、怒容可掬 - Means 'seeing that the face was still alive-looking, with a visible expression of anger.'
  • 报仇雪愤，何其神也 - Means 'avenging and clearing one's anger, how divine it is!'
  • 噫嘻 - An interjection expressing surprise or admiration.
  • 阳有官刑，阴有冥罚 - Means 'there is official punishment in the light, and divine retribution in the darkness.'
  • 自古及今，焉有杀人而能漏网者哉 - Means 'from ancient times to the present, how can there be anyone who kills and escapes unpunished?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 里乘子 - A title or name, possibly a fictional or historical figure, used here as a narrator.
  • 某甲 - A placeholder name for a person, often used in classical texts to refer to an anonymous individual.
  • 三老 - Refers to respected elders in a village or community, often involved in local governance and decision-making.
  • 官刑 - Refers to official or legal punishment.
  • 冥罚 - Refers to divine or supernatural punishment.

  • 泰興 - Taixing, a county in Jiangsu Province, China, where Wu He Fu was officially registered.

  • 督學 - Provincial governor of education, responsible for overseeing education and examinations in a province.
  • 滇甫 - Yunnan Province, located in the southwestern part of China, known for its ethnic diversity and historical significance.
  • 回民 - Hui people, a predominantly Muslim ethnic group in China, historically associated with certain rebellions and conflicts.
  • 鹽巡道 - Salt inspectorate, a government office responsible for the administration and taxation of salt.
  • 永昌 - Yongchang, a historical prefecture in Yunnan Province, known for its strategic and cultural importance.
  • 天朝 - A term for the Chinese empire, often used in historical contexts to refer to the Qing dynasty or earlier Chinese dynasties.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 幸得保全首领 - Fortunately managed to preserve our lives.
  • 犯上作乱 - To defy authority and cause disorder.
  • 其何面目以见公乎 - How can we face the public?
  • 督学浙江 - Overseeing education in Zhejiang.
  • 按临嘉兴时 - When inspecting Jiaxing.
  • 海盐某生以非罪系狱 - A certain student from Haixi was unjustly imprisoned.
  • 受刑甚酷 - Suffered very severe punishment.
  • 公廉知其诬 - The official discovered that it was a false accusation.
  • 点名至生 - Called out the student's name.
  • 提调官以系狱对 - The examination official answered that the student was imprisoned.
  • 公叱曰 - The official scolded, saying.

  • 应详准学使褫革 - Should be investigated and confirmed by the provincial examination supervisor before being dismissed.
  • 今未详革而敢擅下于狱 - Now, without proper investigation, how dare you arbitrarily imprison him in prison?
  • 是何例也 - What precedent is this?
  • 如某生不来 - If the student does not come.
  • 老夫当停试奏办 - I will stop the examination and report this matter.
  • 提调大恐 - The examination official was greatly frightened.
  • 亟谕邑令出生于狱 - Quickly ordered the county magistrate to release the student from prison.
  • 比生至 - When the student arrived.
  • 发蓬面垢 - With disheveled hair and dirty face.
  • 形同重犯 - Looks like a serious criminal.
  • 公太息抚慰之曰 - The official sighed deeply and comforted him, saying.

  • 种种皆老夫教令不行所至也 - All of this is due to my instructions not being carried out as ordered.
